@graph.name: Land Rover Defender OCTA Engine Era Map
Land Rover Defender OCTA Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender OCTA engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Defender OCTA engine (S68B44B) typically costs £10,000–£13,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,000. These are high-cost, specialist-only replacements — the OCTA's BMW-sourced V8 and 48V MHEV system require Land Rover or BMW specialist expertise.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender OCTA V8 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S68B44B is BMW's latest-generation 4.4L twin-turbo V8, shared with the Range Rover Sport SV. It has proven robust in BMW M models, but the Defender OCTA is a low-volume, high-performance variant launched in 2024. Early data suggests strong reliability, though long-term owner experience is still emerging. The 48V mild-hybrid system adds complexity — maintain strictly to manufacturer schedules.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Defender OCTA?

acceptedAnswer.text: 48V mild-hybrid system issues (battery/module faults), cooling system stress on the high-output V8, and high-performance brake and drivetrain wear due to the vehicle's 2.5-tonne weight and 635PS output. The OCTA's 0–60mph time of 3.8 seconds puts immense stress on every component.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Defender OCTA engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OCTA starts from £145,300 — a £10,000–£13,000 engine replacement is 7–9% of the vehicle's value. This is almost always worth it for a well-maintained example. Our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold would only apply if the vehicle is heavily damaged or very high mileage — unlikely on a 2024+ model.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Defender OCTA engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OCTA uses a single engine code: S68B44B — BMW's 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 with 48V mild-hybrid. This is shared with the Range Rover Sport SV. The code is stamped on the engine block; your VIN and vehicle documentation will also confirm the specification.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 48V MHEV system, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if diagnosed early. Battery module replacement (£800–£2,500) is the most common repair. The DC-DC converter and control modules can also fail. The system is well-understood from BMW applications, but requires specialist diagnostic equipment. At the first sign of 48V warnings, book an inspection immediately — ignoring warnings can lead to more expensive control module failures.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Defender OCTA Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current S68B44B generation — the OCTA is a single-generation model from 2024 onward. All pricing is indicative; actual costs vary by mileage, condition and workshop location.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Defender OCTA V8 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £80–£120/hr (independent Land Rover specialist) or £140–£190/hr (London/SE dealer).

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Defender OCTA — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Vehicle under warranty or high-value — always worth fixing
Straightforward repair on an otherwise healthy engine
Long-term ownership; BMW S-engine timing chains are robust but labour-intensive
Replacement often £10,000–£14,000 — assess vehicle value carefully

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Defender OCTA? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Land Rover service history with BMW MHEV-specific maintenance logged
* Low mileage (under 20,000) with documented running-in procedure followed
* 48V system health check completed by a Land Rover specialist
* OCTA Mode and 6D Dynamics suspension function tested
avoidIf: * Any 48V electrical warnings present without a documented fix
* Evidence of coolant loss or overheating (cooling system stress)
* Poor service history — this engine demands precise oil specification
* High mileage without documented major service (brakes, transmission, diff)

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Defender OCTA is a landmark vehicle — the most powerful Defender ever built — but it's also a complex piece of engineering. The BMW-sourced S68 V8 is a proven unit, but its 48V MHEV system and the vehicle's 2.5-tonne weight place unusual stress on every component. Buy on documented history; maintain religiously; and accept that running costs are commensurate with a £145,000+ vehicle.

cta.label: → Compare Defender OCTA engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Defender OCTA (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup; the S68 should fire instantly with a distinctive V8 bark
* Service History — Including BMW S68-specific oil specification (LL-04 or later) and MHEV battery health checks
* 48V System Check — Any warnings on the dash; battery health; start-stop function working correctly
* Coolant Level — Check for any loss; the cooling system works hard in this high-performance V8
* Turbo Response — Test acceleration; any hesitation, lag, or whistling indicates turbo or wastegate issues
* Oil Consumption — Monitor between services; S68 engines are generally robust but can consume oil in hard use
* Brake & Drivetrain — Check for judder, clunks, or whine — the OCTA's weight and power stress these components
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (MHEV systems can trigger warnings)
